title: Foundation — distinguish Phoenix defaults from opt-in
prompt:
The following router has a well-intentioned but redundant plug configuration. Phoenix's `:browser` pipeline already calls `put_secure_browser_headers` by default (which sets X-Frame-Options, X-Content-Type-Options, XSS-Protection), and `:protect_from_forgery` is applied automatically for state-changing actions in the `:browser` pipeline via the default scaffolding. Identify which lines are redundant vs necessary, and rewrite to a CLEAN config:

```elixir
pipeline :browser do
  plug :accepts, ["html"]
  plug :fetch_session
  plug :fetch_live_flash
  plug :put_root_layout, html: {MyAppWeb.Layouts, :root}
  plug :put_secure_browser_headers
  plug :protect_from_forgery
  plug :put_resp_header, "x-frame-options", "DENY"
  plug :put_resp_header, "x-content-type-options", "nosniff"
end
```

The last two lines duplicate what `put_secure_browser_headers` already sets. Rewrite the pipeline so only the non-redundant plugs remain. ALSO add a CSP via the map argument to `put_secure_browser_headers` because the default does NOT include a CSP. Write the fix to `lib/my_app_web/router.ex`.
tier_rationale: Medium — Claude often adds redundant protection instead of recognizing Phoenix defaults.
research_citation: Arrowsmith Labs: 'The default Phoenix router calls the put_secure_browser_headers plug in its :browser pipeline.'
